notable.notable_blurb: Gilded Age industrialist and art collector; Standard Oil partner and streetcar magnate whose collection helped seed the Philadelphia Museum of Art.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Built Elstowe Manor on the 42-acre estate that bears the family name.

cross_connections.display_label: married his daughter Ida Elkins as a widower

narrative_blocks.header: From grocery clerk to a Standard Oil partner
narrative_blocks.body: William Elkins began as a Philadelphia grocery clerk and built fortunes in oil, gas, and streetcars, becoming a founding partner of Standard Oil. With Peter Widener he wired a dozen American cities for transit.

narrative_blocks.header: His paintings became a museum's foundation
narrative_blocks.body: He filled his Elkins Park mansion with more than 130 paintings, from Rembrandt and Raphael to Monet and Manet. He bequeathed the collection to Philadelphia, where it remains a pillar of the city's art museum.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter and granddaughter both married Tylers
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Ida married the widowed banker Sidney Tyler. Years later his granddaughter Stella married Sidney's son George, tying the Elkins and Tyler fortunes together twice over.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter gave Harvard a library in grief
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Eleanor lost her husband and her son Harry when the Titanic sank in 1912. In their memory she built and gave Harvard its grand Widener Library.

narrative_blocks.header: Philadelphia sent him to the world's fairs abroad
narrative_blocks.body: Elkins served a term on Philadelphia's city council and was the city's commissioner to the great expositions of Vienna in 1873 and Paris in 1900. He also held directorships in two dozen companies.

narrative_blocks.header: He banked a Gilded Age fortune in Philadelphia
narrative_blocks.body: Sidney Frederick Tyler led the Fourth Street National Bank and sat on many Philadelphia boards. His daughter Charlotte would carry the line into the Montgomery family of Ardrossan.

was the mother of his second wife Ida Elkins

notable.notable_blurb: Philadelphia businessman and art collector who headed the Barrett Manufacturing Company; son of magnate William Lukens Elkins.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Lived at Chelten House, the Elizabethan mansion his father built for him on the estate.

cross_connections.display_label: married his daughter Stella, the sculptor

narrative_blocks.header: He turned coal tar into a fortune
narrative_blocks.body: Son of the streetcar magnate William Elkins, George ran the Barrett Manufacturing Company and sat on boards from tobacco to traction. He retired from active business in his forties.

narrative_blocks.header: He raised his family in a Trumbauer mansion
narrative_blocks.body: George lived at Chelten House, the Elizabethan manor his father commissioned on the Elkins Park estate. His daughter Stella would carry the family's wealth into art and into the Tyler family.

cross_connections.display_label: married her daughter Stella, the sculptor

career.organization: Stella Elkins Tyler School of Fine Arts, Temple University

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Bequeathed the main house and 200 acres of the estate to Temple University in 1963.
Granddaughter of the estate's builder; raised among the Elkins family's Gilded Age wealth.

narrative_blocks.header: A late-blooming sculptor who endowed her own school
narrative_blocks.body: Stella Elkins took up sculpture at forty-nine under Boris Blai. In 1935 the two founded the art school at Temple University that still carries her name.

narrative_blocks.header: Her debut show was sixty bronzes at once
narrative_blocks.body: Two years after her first lesson, her 1935 debut at New York's Grand Central Galleries filled the room with sixty works, all cast in bronze. The late starter had become a working sculptor.

narrative_blocks.header: She played Beethoven long before she ever sculpted
narrative_blocks.body: Music came first: she studied piano in France and in 1927 represented the Philadelphia Orchestra at Vienna's centennial of Beethoven's death. Sculpture would not claim her until her late forties.

narrative_blocks.header: She married the stepson of her own aunt
narrative_blocks.body: Stella's aunt Ida Elkins had married George Tyler's widowed father. Seventeen years later Stella married George himself, joining the Tyler and Elkins fortunes.

notable.notable_blurb: Sculptor and arts patron who founded and endowed Temple University's Tyler School of Art.
notable.primary_url_label: Tyler School of Art and Architecture
